html:
Roofing Gardner's Furniture-Era Buildings and Route 68 Plazas

Gardner built its identity on furniture manufacturing, and the giant chair on Elm Street still marks that history for anyone driving through. The commercial roofs we work on here split between old furniture-plant buildings now used for storage or light industry, healthcare facilities near Heywood Hospital, and retail plazas along Route 68 and Route 140.

Former Furniture-Plant Roof Decks

Gardner's furniture manufacturers needed large, open floor plates with minimal interior columns, which means the roof decks over these buildings often span wide bays on heavy timber or steel truss framing. Where these buildings have shifted to storage, distribution, or light manufacturing use, the roof itself frequently hasn't been touched since the furniture operation closed, and deck condition varies a lot from one bay to the next depending on past leak history.

Dust collection systems and old finishing-room exhaust penetrations are common leftovers on these roofs, capped off but not always flashed properly when they were decommissioned. We check every abandoned penetration on a furniture-era building rather than assuming a capped stack is a sealed stack.

Wood dust and resin residue from decades of finishing operations can also still be present in roof drains and gutters on buildings that haven't had a full roof-level cleaning, which affects drainage more than most owners expect walking the roof for the first time.

Some of these buildings also carry old wood-frame monitor or clerestory sections built to vent finishing fumes before modern exhaust systems existed. Where those sections are no longer functional, we evaluate whether it makes more sense to rebuild them as standard low-slope roof or maintain the original profile, a decision that depends heavily on how the space below is currently used.

Healthcare and Medical Office Roofs Near Heywood Hospital

Medical office buildings and clinical space near Heywood Hospital carry rooftop equipment that runs continuously: imaging equipment condensers, backup generators, and HVAC sized for tighter humidity and temperature control than a typical office. Roof penetrations for this equipment need flashing built to handle both the added weight and the vibration of units that rarely shut off.

We also plan healthcare-adjacent roof work around continuity of care, scheduling noisy or disruptive work in phases so clinical operations underneath aren't interrupted.

Backup generator enclosures and their roof-level fuel or exhaust connections are another detail we check closely on medical buildings, since a generator that only runs during a power outage still needs its roof penetration sealed to the same standard as equipment running every day. A failure there tends to go unnoticed until the generator is actually needed.

Route 68 and Route 140 Retail Plazas

The retail plazas along Gardner's main commercial corridors are mostly low-slope, single-story buildings with straightforward roof plans, but winter performance is the recurring issue. Central Massachusetts snow load combined with poor insulation on some of the older plaza roofs leads to interior heat loss that melts snow unevenly, which then refreezes at the eave and backs water up under the membrane.

On a typical plaza roof assessment we look at:

  • Insulation thickness relative to current energy code minimums
  • Drain and scupper capacity against roof area
  • Parapet coping and wall cap condition
  • Tenant HVAC curb flashing and unit age
  • Rooftop signage penetrations
  • Membrane seam condition at high-traffic access points

Budgeting Reroofs Across Gardner's Building Types

Former furniture-plant buildings with wide truss spans generally do well with a mechanically attached 60-mil TPO system, since the open bays don't have the dense penetration count that would favor a fully adhered membrane. Where deck repair is needed over old dust-collection penetrations, that repair cost gets scoped separately from the membrane bid so owners see exactly what's driving the number.

Healthcare and medical office roofs typically justify the added cost of a fully adhered PVC or EPDM system given the penetration density and the priority on minimizing future leak risk near clinical space, even though the material cost per square foot runs higher than a mechanically attached alternative.

Route 68 and Route 140 plaza roofs are usually the most straightforward jobs in town, and insulation upgrades to R-25 or better during a reroof often pay for themselves faster here than the membrane choice itself, given how much heat loss we find on older plaza buildings. Anchor tenants with larger footprints sometimes fund their own portion of a plaza reroof independent of the property owner, and coordinating that split scope so the finished roof still reads as one continuous system is worth planning for before work starts rather than after.

​

Questions Gardner Building Owners Ask

What should I expect finding on a former furniture-plant roof?

Abandoned dust-collection and exhaust penetrations that weren't always flashed properly when capped, plus deck condition that can vary significantly bay to bay depending on past leak history.

Do medical office buildings need special roof detailing?

Yes. Continuous-run equipment like imaging condensers and generators needs curb flashing built for sustained vibration and load, not standard retail HVAC detailing.

Why do plaza roofs in Gardner ice dam more than expected?

Older buildings with thin original insulation lose interior heat unevenly, melting roof snow that refreezes at the eave and backs up under the membrane.

Can wide-span truss roofs support a heavier membrane system?

Most can, but we verify truss and deck condition first, especially on buildings with a manufacturing history where load paths may have been altered for old equipment.

How do you schedule roof work near an operating clinical facility?

We phase disruptive work and coordinate timing with facility staff so noise and access don't interfere with patient care underneath.
